title: Iran Strikes Gulf States as Ukraine Hits St Petersburg on Putin's Forum Opening Day
subhead: The Iran-US war entered a dangerous new phase with IRGC missile strikes on Kuwait and Bahrain, while Ukraine launched a major drone attack on St Petersburg as Putin's flagship economic forum opened. The parallel escalations highlight the interconnected instability facing the US and its allies.

sections.heading: Iran Strikes Kuwait and Bahrain, Expanding the War
sections.body:
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) launched missile and drone attacks on Kuwait International Airport and targets in Bahrain on Tuesday, killing at least one person and wounding more than 60. The IRGC said the strikes were retaliation for US attacks on an Iranian oil tanker and Qeshm Island in the Persian Gulf. Kuwait reported 60+ wounded, with flights disrupted at the airport.

The strikes mark the first direct IRGC attacks on Gulf Arab states since the Iran-US war began on February 28. They signal that Tehran is willing to broaden the conflict beyond American targets, potentially drawing US allies into the fighting. The US military confirmed strikes on Qeshm Island but did not immediately comment on the Gulf state attacks.

According to Al Jazeera, the attacks raise questions about whether the Gulf war is escalating again after weeks of relative calm. The Strait of Hormuz remains under Iranian control, with approximately 20,000 sailors still stranded on cargo ships unable to transit the vital waterway.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran's decision to strike Kuwait and Bahrain represents a significant escalation beyond the US-Iran bilateral conflict. It tests US security commitments to Gulf allies and risks drawing more states into direct participation. The attacks also demonstrate that Tehran retains significant missile and drone capabilities despite months of US and Israeli strikes on Iranian infrastructure.

sections.heading: Iran-Backed Iraqi Militias Begin Disarmament Under US Pressure
sections.body:
Multiple Iran-backed militias in Iraq announced they will sever ties with the Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF) and begin procedures to place weapons under state control. The groups include Kataib al-Imam Ali and Asaib Ahl al-Haq, according to regional reporting tracked by the subagent. The move comes amid intense US pressure on Iraq's new Prime Minister Ali al-Zaidi, who proposed linking US investment to the disarmament of armed factions.

Muqtada al-Sadr's Saraya al-Salam also pledged to place itself under state control. However, Harakat Hezbollah al-Nujaba reportedly opposes the government initiative, revealing fractures among pro-Iran armed groups in Iraq. The developments suggest that the combination of US military pressure on Iran and diplomatic leverage on Baghdad is producing results on the ground.
sections.whyItMatters: The partial disarmament of Iran-backed Iraqi militias would remove a significant pressure vector from Tehran's regional toolkit. If successful, it could serve as a model for reducing Iranian influence in other theaters. However, the opposition from Nujaba shows the process will face armed resistance from hardline factions.

sections.heading: Ukraine Hits St Petersburg as Putin's Economic Forum Opens
sections.body:
Ukrainian drones struck St Petersburg on Tuesday, coinciding with the opening of Vladimir Putin's flagship St Petersburg International Economic Forum (SPIEF). According to BBC and regional reporting, an oil terminal and the Kronstadt naval base were hit, with the Russian corvette Boikiy reportedly damaged. Russian authorities said 59 drones were shot down over the city, Pulkovo Airport was temporarily closed, and mobile internet was disrupted. There were no reported casualties.

President Volodymyr Zelensky called the strikes 'long-range sanctions' and framed them as economic warfare against Russia's war machine. The timing—during Putin's premier economic showcase—underscored Ukraine's ability to reach deep into Russian territory and disrupt Moscow's narrative of stability.

The attack came one day after Russia launched 700+ missiles and drones across Ukraine on June 2, killing 22 people including women and children in one of the largest bombardments since 2022. The cycle of escalation shows no sign of breaking.
sections.whyItMatters: The St Petersburg strike demonstrates that Ukraine retains significant drone strike capability and is willing to escalate against Russian symbolic and economic targets. The attack during SPIEF—the highest-profile Russian economic event—directly challenged Moscow's efforts to project business-as-usual to international partners.

sections.heading: Russian Media Publishes Doubts About Winning the War
sections.body:
Russian publications this week aired unusual public debate about whether the war in Ukraine can be won. According to BBC reporting, the journal Russia In Global Affairs and the newspaper Moskovsky Komsomolets published articles questioning the war's trajectory. One article in Moskovsky Komsomolets argued that 'lost wars led to breakthroughs'—suggesting that accepting defeat could lead to national renewal. That article was later deleted and now returns a 404 error.

The public airing of such views marks a shift in Russia's domestic information environment. After years of tight control over war coverage, the appearance of elite debate—however brief—suggests growing internal pressure. Putin remains publicly uncompromising, demanding Ukraine cede the entire Donbas region. The 'Spirit of Anchorage' phrase from the 2025 US-Russia summit has been disavowed by Putin's aide Yuri Ushakov.

Russia's economy continues to face significant strain: budget deficits are growing, stagnation is setting in, and sanctions continue to bite. The St Petersburg Economic Forum is notably subdued compared to past years—130 countries are attending, but high-profile Western attendees are absent. The US delegation is led by Rodney Mims Cook Jr, who is overseeing Trump's White House ballroom project.
sections.whyItMatters: The appearance of elite debate about the war's viability—even if quickly suppressed—signals potential cracks in Moscow's domestic consensus. Economic pressure and military stagnation are producing visible costs, though Putin's position remains strong. The deleted article suggests hardliners remain unwilling to tolerate public dissent.

sections.heading: Israel Expands Permanent Military Presence in Gaza
sections.body:
Satellite imagery analyzed by Al Jazeera reveals that Israel has built approximately 40 military outposts in Gaza, with eight constructed entirely after the October 2025 ceasefire. One base was reportedly built atop a bulldozed cemetery in Khan Younis. Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu confirmed directives to permanently seize the vast majority of Gaza, stating Israel controls 60 percent of the territory.

The expansion of permanent military infrastructure contradicts the framework of a temporary occupation and signals Israel's intent to maintain long-term security control over Gaza. The construction continues despite ongoing ceasefire negotiations and international pressure for a political solution.

Meanwhile, the Israel-Hezbollah ceasefire remains partial. While Israel has agreed not to bomb Beirut and Hezbollah has agreed not to attack Israel, Israeli strikes continue in southern Lebanon. Iran has warned that Israeli action in Lebanon jeopardizes the broader Iran-US ceasefire discussions.
sections.whyItMatters: Israel's permanent military entrenchment in Gaza represents a fundamental shift in the conflict's trajectory. The construction of bases on Gazan territory signals that the war's end state will not include full Palestinian sovereignty. The linkage between Israeli action in Lebanon and US-Iran negotiations shows how regional conflicts remain interconnected.

sections.heading: US Tariffs and Trade Tensions Persist
sections.body:
Trade tensions continue as the US maintains and in some cases expands tariff regimes. The broader economic disruption from the Iran-US conflict has affected global shipping routes, energy prices, and supply chains. The Strait of Hormuz blockade continues to impact oil and gas flows, with alternative routes through the Caspian and Eastern Mediterranean gaining importance.

The European Union has launched initiatives for tech sovereignty and continues to discuss whether to exclude Ukrainian men aged 23-60 from temporary protection extensions—a move that would effectively push military-age males back toward conscription in Ukraine.
sections.whyItMatters: The combination of trade tensions, energy disruptions, and shifting refugee policies creates compounding pressure on multiple fronts. The EU's potential change to Ukrainian refugee protection could significantly affect Ukraine's mobilization capacity and the demographic sustainability of its war effort.

bottomLine: June 3rd saw parallel escalations across the two major conflicts absorbing US attention. Iran's strikes on Kuwait and Bahrain marked a new phase in the Gulf war—one that directly tests US security commitments to regional allies. At the same time, Ukraine's drone attack on St Petersburg during Putin's economic forum demonstrated Kyiv's ability to impose costs on the Russian homeland even as Moscow struggles to translate military pressure into political gains. The partial disarmament of Iran-backed Iraqi militias offers a potential off-ramp for one pressure vector, but it faces opposition from hardline factions. The Israel-Gaza conflict continues to entrench, with permanent military infrastructure signaling long-term occupation. All three theaters—Iran, Ukraine, Gaza—remain locked in escalatory cycles with no clear diplomatic resolution in sight.
